revision.comment: Page created.
revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in Hidaka, Japan, dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by an emissary from the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Goguryeo prince who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/>
==Referneces== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Places of worship in Japan]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]]

revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>

==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Goguryeo prince who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/>
==Referneces== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Places of worship in Japan]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]]

revision.comment: Following the annexation of Korea by Japan, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including Governor-General of Korea Jirō Minami, for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.[2]
revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by an emissary from the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref>
Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Goguryeo prince who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/>
==Referneces== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Places of worship in Japan]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]]

revision.comment: In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by an emissary from the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref>
Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Goguryeo prince who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/>
==Referneces== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese building and structure stubs]] [[Category:Places of worship in Japan]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]]

revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by an emissary from the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref>
Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Goguryeo prince who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/>
==Referneces== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by an emissary from the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref>
Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually.<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Goguryeo prince who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/>
==Referneces== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>

Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually.<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Goguryeo prince who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/>
==Referneces== {{reflist}}

The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
[[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]]

Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>

==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually.<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)) who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/> Its priests are believed to be descendants of the Koma family.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.ca/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&pg=PA45&lpg=PA45&dq=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&source=bl&ots=tIUmVHsosc&sig=BYSO0nAZTq3V9Gxjk67lXiPPd6A&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwi2rKrCm7XWAhWHwYMKHanvDBkQ6AEIXTAM#v=onepage&q=%22jakko%22%20%22koma%22&f=false |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}}</ref>

revision.comment: /* History */ The Prince was dispatched to Japan to garner support for the Goruryeo Kingdom following an invasion by Silla and Tang dynasty China, but ended up settling in Japan, along with approximately 1,800 other Koreans.[2]
revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
[[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]]
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref> The Prince was dispatched to Japan to garner support for the Goruryeo Kingdom following an invasion by [[Silla]] and [[Tang dynasty]] [[China]], but ended up settling in Japan, along with approximately 1,800 other Koreans.<ref name="KF">{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}}</ref>
Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually.<ref name="Spaces"/>

The '''Koma Shrine''' ( 高麗神社) is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
[[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]]
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref> The Prince was dispatched to Japan to garner support for the Goruryeo Kingdom following an invasion by [[Silla]] and [[Tang dynasty]] [[China]], but ended up settling in Japan, along with approximately 1,800 other Koreans.<ref name="KF">{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}}</ref>
Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually.<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)) who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/> Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death.<ref name="KF"/>
Its priests are believed to be descendants of the Koma family.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.ca/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&pg=PA45&lpg=PA45&dq=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&source=bl&ots=tIUmVHsosc&sig=BYSO0nAZTq3V9Gxjk67lXiPPd6A&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwi2rKrCm7XWAhWHwYMKHanvDBkQ6AEIXTAM#v=onepage&q=%22jakko%22%20%22koma%22&f=false |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}}</ref> The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces"/>
==Referneces== {{reflist}}

revision.comment: /* History */
revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' ( 高麗神社) is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=[[The Associated Press]] |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
[[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]]
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref> The Prince was dispatched to Japan to garner support for the Goruryeo Kingdom following an invasion by [[Silla]] and [[Tang dynasty]] [[China]], but ended up settling in Japan, along with approximately 1,800 other Koreans.<ref name="KF">{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}}</ref> Japanese emperor entitled prince Yakgwang as state king position and the people governed the territory allocated until about 13C.
Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually.<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)) who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/> Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death.<ref name="KF"/>
Its priests are believed to be descendants of the Koma family.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.ca/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&pg=PA45&lpg=PA45&dq=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&source=bl&ots=tIUmVHsosc&sig=BYSO0nAZTq3V9Gxjk67lXiPPd6A&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwi2rKrCm7XWAhWHwYMKHanvDBkQ6AEIXTAM#v=onepage&q=%22jakko%22%20%22koma%22&f=false |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}}</ref> The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces"/>
==References== {{reflist}}

Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually.<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)) who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/> Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death.<ref name="KF"/>
Its priests are believed to be descendants of the Koma family.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&pg=PA45&lpg=PA45&dq=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22#q=%22jakko%22%20%22koma%22 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}}</ref> The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces"/>
==References== {{reflist}}

Its priests are believed to be descendants of the Koma family.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}}</ref> The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces"/>

revision.text:
The '''Koma Shrine''' (高麗神社) is a shrine in [[Hidaka, Saitama]], dedicated to early [[Koreans|Korean]] settlers of Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}}</ref>
[[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]]
==History== The shrine was founded in 716 CE by Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Goguryeo]] kingdom of [[Korea]].<ref name="Yonhap">{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}}</ref> The Prince was dispatched to Japan to garner support for the Goruryeo Kingdom following an invasion by [[Silla]] and [[Tang dynasty]] [[China]], but ended up settling in Japan, along with approximately 1,800 other Koreans.<ref name="KF">{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}}</ref> Japanese emperor entitled prince Yakgwang as state king position and the people governed the territory allocated until about 13C.
Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]], the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces">{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}}</ref> In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]].<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually.<ref name="Spaces"/>
==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)) who arrived in Japan fleeing political unrest in Korea.<ref name="Yonhap"/> Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death.<ref name="KF"/>
Its priests are believed to be descendants of the Koma family.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}}</ref> The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō.<ref name="KF"/><ref name="Spaces"/>

revision.comment: infobox, expand
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The work "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]], specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]], and the main ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo. {{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.ancient.eu/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=Ancient History Encyclopedia|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time..{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]]

Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]]

revision.comment: +{{[[Template:Authority control|Authority control]]}} ([[d:Q10855289|3 IDs]] from [[Wikidata]]), [[WP:GenFixes]] on
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The work "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]], specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]], and the main ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The work "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]], specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]], and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to spread Goguryeo influence to the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]]

In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]]

[[Category:Beppyo shrines]]{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The work "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]], specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]], and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=[[Edition Axel Menges]]}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]]

The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]]

revision.comment: /* top */ not sourced, what does "Koma, pronounced as Koryō in Japanese is the ancient Japanese name for Korea" even mean
revision.text:
[[Category:Beppyo shrines]]{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The work "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]]
