{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The work "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] {

revision.comment: added [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] using [[WP:HC|HotCat]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] {

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] {

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[category:dd]] {

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[en:Koma Shrine]]{

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[en:Koma Shrine]]{

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Koma Shrine]]{

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[category:Immigration to Japan]] [[category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Koma Shrine]]{

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|53|54.9|N|139|19|22.1|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Koma no ko ni kishijakkō | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[Korean Peninsula]] and the southern and central parts of [[Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[Baekje]] and [[Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[Asuka period]] [[Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[Hannō]], [[Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[Koma clan]] until the end of the [[Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[Governor-General of Korea]] [[Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[JR East]] [[Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[silkworms]] and [[mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] {{Authority control}} [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[en:Koma Shrine]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[en:Koma Shrine]]{
