It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Koma Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = File:Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.898583|N|139.322806|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Koma no Jakkō|WD=Q8010424}} | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hidaka|WD=Q905721}} }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Hidaka, Saitama|Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[:en:Korea|Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and the southern and central parts of [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[:en:Inner Mongolia|Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[:en:Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[:en:Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[:en:Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[:en:Hannō|Hannō]], [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[:en:Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] until the end of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] [[:en:Jirō Minami|Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[:en:Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[:en:Hideo Kodama|Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[:en:Eiji Yoshikawa|Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[:en:Ango Sakaguchi|Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[:en:Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Komagawa Station|Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Koma Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[:en:Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[:en:Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[:en:Hannō|Hannō]], [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[:en:Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] until the end of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] [[:en:Jirō Minami|Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[:en:Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[:en:Hideo Kodama|Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[:en:Eiji Yoshikawa|Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[:en:Ango Sakaguchi|Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[:en:Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Komagawa Station|Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Koma Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = File:Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.898583|N|139.322806|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Koma no Jakkō|WD=Q8010424}} | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hidaka|WD=Q905721}} }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Hidaka, Saitama|Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[:en:Korea|Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and the southern and central parts of [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[:en:Inner Mongolia|Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[:en:Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[:en:Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[:en:Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[:en:Hannō|Hannō]], [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[:en:Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] until the end of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] [[:en:Jirō Minami|Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[:en:Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[:en:Hideo Kodama|Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[:en:Eiji Yoshikawa|Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[:en:Ango Sakaguchi|Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[:en:Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Komagawa Station|Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Koma Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Koma Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

revision.comment: Bot: reorganize categories (alphabetical, deduplicated, moved to bottom)
revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = File:Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.898583|N|139.322806|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Koma no Jakkō|WD=Q8010424}} | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hidaka|WD=Q905721}} }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Hidaka, Saitama|Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[:en:Korea|Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and the southern and central parts of [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[:en:Inner Mongolia|Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[:en:Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[:en:Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[:en:Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[:en:Hannō|Hannō]], [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[:en:Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] until the end of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] [[:en:Jirō Minami|Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[:en:Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[:en:Hideo Kodama|Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[:en:Eiji Yoshikawa|Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[:en:Ango Sakaguchi|Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[:en:Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Komagawa Station|Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[en:Koma Shrine]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = File:Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.898583|N|139.322806|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Koma no Jakkō|WD=Q8010424}} | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hidaka|WD=Q905721}} }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Hidaka, Saitama|Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[:en:Korea|Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and the southern and central parts of [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[:en:Inner Mongolia|Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[:en:Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[:en:Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[:en:Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[:en:Hannō|Hannō]], [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[:en:Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] until the end of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] [[:en:Jirō Minami|Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[:en:Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[:en:Hideo Kodama|Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[:en:Eiji Yoshikawa|Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[:en:Ango Sakaguchi|Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[:en:Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Komagawa Station|Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[en:Koma Shrine]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[:en:Inner Mongolia|Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[:en:Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[:en:Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[:en:Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[:en:Hannō|Hannō]], [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[:en:Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] until the end of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] [[:en:Jirō Minami|Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[:en:Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[:en:Hideo Kodama|Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[:en:Eiji Yoshikawa|Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[:en:Ango Sakaguchi|Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[:en:Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Komagawa Station|Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ [[en:Koma Shrine]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

高麗神社 | image = File:Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.898583|N|139.322806|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Koma no Jakkō|WD=Q8010424}} | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hidaka|WD=Q905721}} }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Hidaka, Saitama|Hidaka, Saitama]]. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for [[:en:Korea|Korea]],{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]],{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and the southern and central parts of [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]]. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern [[:en:Inner Mongolia|Inner Mongolia]].{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]], Goguryeo was one of the [[:en:Three Kingdoms of Korea|Three Kingdoms of Korea]]. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in [[:en:Military history of Goguryeo|military conflict]] with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong]] saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, [[:en:Bojang of Goguryeo|Bojang]], sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]].{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and [[:en:Tsurugashima, Saitama|Tsurugashima]], as well as parts of [[:en:Hannō|Hannō]], [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]], [[:en:Iruma, Saitama|Iruma]] and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] until the end of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|annexation of Korea by Japan]] in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] [[:en:Jirō Minami|Jirō Minami]], for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-[[:en:Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|Minister of Colonial Affairs]] [[:en:Hideo Kodama|Hideo Kodama]]. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as [[:en:Eiji Yoshikawa|Eiji Yoshikawa]] and [[:en:Ango Sakaguchi|Ango Sakaguchi]]. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the [[:en:Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs]]. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Komagawa Station|Komagawa Station]]. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the [[:en:Koma clan|Koma clan]] (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|''irimoya'']]-gabled building and was designated as a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ [[en:Koma Shrine]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]]

Following the {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=annexation of Korea by Japan}} in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] {{ill|Jirō Minami|en|Jirō Minami|WD=Q712893|lt=Jirō Minami}}, for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-{{ill|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|WD=Q3315484|lt=Minister of Colonial Affairs}} {{ill|Hideo Kodama|en|Hideo Kodama|WD=Q859979|lt=Hideo Kodama}}. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as {{ill|Eiji Yoshikawa|en|Eiji Yoshikawa|WD=Q221957|lt=Eiji Yoshikawa}} and {{ill|Ango Sakaguchi|en|Ango Sakaguchi|WD=Q132111|lt=Ango Sakaguchi}}. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the {{ill|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|en|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|WD=Q9405654|lt=South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs}}. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Komagawa Station|en|Komagawa Station|WD=Q910599|lt=Komagawa Station}}. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|WD=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan}} (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=''irimoya''}}-gabled building and was designated as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ [[en:Koma Shrine]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855289]] Wikidata name: Koma Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = File:Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.898583|N|139.322806|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Koma no Jakkō|WD=Q8010424}} | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hidaka|WD=Q905721}} }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the city of {{ill|Hidaka, Saitama|en|Hidaka, Saitama|WD=Q905721|lt=Hidaka, Saitama}}. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}},{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}},{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and the southern and central parts of {{ill|Manchuria|en|Manchuria|WD=Q81126|lt=Manchuria}}. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern {{ill|Inner Mongolia|en|Inner Mongolia|WD=Q41079|lt=Inner Mongolia}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, Goguryeo was one of the {{ill|Three Kingdoms of Korea|en|Three Kingdoms of Korea|WD=Q165292|lt=Three Kingdoms of Korea}}. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in {{ill|Military history of Goguryeo|en|Military history of Goguryeo|WD=Q17052201|lt=military conflict}} with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|en|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|WD=Q9703|lt=Emperor Gaozong}} saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, {{ill|Bojang of Goguryeo|en|Bojang of Goguryeo|WD=Q498974|lt=Bojang}}, sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}.{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and {{ill|Tsurugashima, Saitama|en|Tsurugashima, Saitama|WD=Q869027|lt=Tsurugashima}}, as well as parts of {{ill|Hannō|en|Hannō|WD=Q850472|lt=Hannō}}, {{ill|Kawagoe, Saitama|en|Kawagoe, Saitama|WD=Q319740|lt=Kawagoe}}, {{ill|Iruma, Saitama|en|Iruma, Saitama|WD=Q696925|lt=Iruma}} and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|WD=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan}} until the end of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=annexation of Korea by Japan}} in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] {{ill|Jirō Minami|en|Jirō Minami|WD=Q712893|lt=Jirō Minami}}, for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-{{ill|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|WD=Q3315484|lt=Minister of Colonial Affairs}} {{ill|Hideo Kodama|en|Hideo Kodama|WD=Q859979|lt=Hideo Kodama}}. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as {{ill|Eiji Yoshikawa|en|Eiji Yoshikawa|WD=Q221957|lt=Eiji Yoshikawa}} and {{ill|Ango Sakaguchi|en|Ango Sakaguchi|WD=Q132111|lt=Ango Sakaguchi}}. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the {{ill|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|en|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|WD=Q9405654|lt=South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs}}. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Komagawa Station|en|Komagawa Station|WD=Q910599|lt=Komagawa Station}}. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|WD=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan}} (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=''irimoya''}}-gabled building and was designated as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ [[en:Koma Shrine]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]]
