revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)]][[en:Koma Shrine]][[fa:معبد کوما]][[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]][[ja:高麗神社]][[ko:고마 신사]][[zh:高麗神社]] {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = File:Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.898583|N|139.322806|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Koma no Jakkō|qid=Q8010424|1=Koma no Jakkō}} | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hidaka|qid=Q905721|1=Hidaka, Saitama}} }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the city of {{ill|Hidaka, Saitama|en|Hidaka, Saitama|qid=Q905721|lt=Hidaka, Saitama|1=Hidaka, Saitama}}. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}},{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}},{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and the southern and central parts of {{ill|Manchuria|en|Manchuria|qid=Q81126|lt=Manchuria|1=Manchuria}}. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern {{ill|Inner Mongolia|en|Inner Mongolia|qid=Q41079|lt=Inner Mongolia|1=Inner Mongolia}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, Goguryeo was one of the {{ill|Three Kingdoms of Korea|en|Three Kingdoms of Korea|qid=Q165292|lt=Three Kingdoms of Korea|1=Three Kingdoms of Korea}}. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in {{ill|Military history of Goguryeo|en|Military history of Goguryeo|qid=Q17052201|lt=military conflict|1=Military history of Goguryeo}} with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|en|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|qid=Q9703|lt=Emperor Gaozong|1=Emperor Gaozong of Tang}} saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, {{ill|Bojang of Goguryeo|en|Bojang of Goguryeo|qid=Q498974|lt=Bojang|1=Bojang of Goguryeo}}, sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}, and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|qid=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}}.{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and {{ill|Tsurugashima, Saitama|en|Tsurugashima, Saitama|qid=Q869027|lt=Tsurugashima|1=Tsurugashima, Saitama}}, as well as parts of {{ill|Hannō|en|Hannō|qid=Q850472|lt=Hannō|1=Hannō}}, {{ill|Kawagoe, Saitama|en|Kawagoe, Saitama|qid=Q319740|lt=Kawagoe|1=Kawagoe, Saitama}}, {{ill|Iruma, Saitama|en|Iruma, Saitama|qid=Q696925|lt=Iruma|1=Iruma, Saitama}} and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|qid=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan|1=Koma clan}} until the end of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|qid=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|qid=Q503585|lt=annexation of Korea by Japan|1=Korea under Japanese rule}} in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] {{ill|Jirō Minami|en|Jirō Minami|qid=Q712893|lt=Jirō Minami|1=Jirō Minami}}, for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-{{ill|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q3315484|lt=Minister of Colonial Affairs|1=Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)}} {{ill|Hideo Kodama|en|Hideo Kodama|qid=Q859979|lt=Hideo Kodama|1=Hideo Kodama}}. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as {{ill|Eiji Yoshikawa|en|Eiji Yoshikawa|qid=Q221957|lt=Eiji Yoshikawa|1=Eiji Yoshikawa}} and {{ill|Ango Sakaguchi|en|Ango Sakaguchi|qid=Q132111|lt=Ango Sakaguchi|1=Ango Sakaguchi}}. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the {{ill|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|en|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|qid=Q9405654|lt=South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs|1=Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)}}. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|qid=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Komagawa Station|en|Komagawa Station|qid=Q910599|lt=Komagawa Station|1=Komagawa Station}}. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|qid=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan|1=Koma clan}} (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|lt=''irimoya''|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}-gabled building and was designated as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ [[en:Koma Shrine]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The area continued to be ruled by the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|qid=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan|1=Koma clan}} until the end of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|qid=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|qid=Q503585|lt=annexation of Korea by Japan|1=Korea under Japanese rule}} in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] {{ill|Jirō Minami|en|Jirō Minami|qid=Q712893|lt=Jirō Minami|1=Jirō Minami}}, for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-{{ill|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q3315484|lt=Minister of Colonial Affairs|1=Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)}} {{ill|Hideo Kodama|en|Hideo Kodama|qid=Q859979|lt=Hideo Kodama|1=Hideo Kodama}}. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as {{ill|Eiji Yoshikawa|en|Eiji Yoshikawa|qid=Q221957|lt=Eiji Yoshikawa|1=Eiji Yoshikawa}} and {{ill|Ango Sakaguchi|en|Ango Sakaguchi|qid=Q132111|lt=Ango Sakaguchi|1=Ango Sakaguchi}}. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the {{ill|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|en|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|qid=Q9405654|lt=South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs|1=Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)}}. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|qid=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Komagawa Station|en|Komagawa Station|qid=Q910599|lt=Komagawa Station|1=Komagawa Station}}. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|qid=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan|1=Koma clan}} (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|lt=''irimoya''|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}-gabled building and was designated as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ [[en:Koma Shrine]] {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} [[fr:Sanctuaire Koma]] [[ja:高麗神社]] [[ko:고마 신사]] [[zh:高麗神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10855289}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Koma Shrine
高麗神社 | image = File:Hidaka Saitama Koma Shrine Shinmon 1.JPG | caption = ''Shinmon'' of Koma Shrine | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.898583|N|139.322806|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Koma Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Koma no Jakkō|qid=Q8010424|1=Koma no Jakkō}} | founded_by = | established = 716 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Hidaka-shi, Saitama-ken | website = {{URL|https://komajinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 19 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hidaka|qid=Q905721|1=Hidaka, Saitama}} }} [[File:Jakko koma clan.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of Jakkō at the shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Koma Shrine'''|高麗神社|Koma Jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the city of {{ill|Hidaka, Saitama|en|Hidaka, Saitama|qid=Q905721|lt=Hidaka, Saitama|1=Hidaka, Saitama}}. The word "Koma", pronounced in Japanese as "Koryō" is the ancient Japanese name for {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}},{{huh|date=August 2022}} specifically the ancient Korean kingdom of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}},{{cn|date=August 2022}} and the main temple ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is the deified Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光), son of the last king of independent Goguryeo.{{cite news |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |author=The Associated Press |author-link=The Associated Press |publisher=[[ABC News]] |title=Japan's emperor visits shrine for ancient Korean settlers |date=September 20, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170921143731/https://abcnews.go.com/International/wireStory/japans-emperor-visits-shrine-ancient-korean-settlers-49971308 |archive-date=September 21, 2017}} ==History== '''Goguryeo''' ({{Korean|hangul=고구려|hanja=高句麗|labels=no}}; {{IPA-ko|ko.ɡu.ɾjʌ|}}), was a Korean kingdom{{cite encyclopedia |url = http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/321038/Koguryo |title = Koguryo |encyclopedia = Encyclopædia Britannica |access-date = October 15, 2013}}{{cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |date=2013 |title=State Formation in Korea: Emerging Elites}}{{cite book |last=Byington |first=Mark |date=2016 |title=The History and Archaeology of the Koguryo Kingdom}}{{cite book |last1=Li |first1=Narangoa |last2=Cribb |first2=Robert |date=2014 |title=Historical Atlas of Northeast Asia}}{{cite thesis | type=PhD | last=Gardiner |first=Kenneth |date=1964 |title=The origin and rise of the Korean kingdom of Koguryo, from the 1st century to A.D. 313 |publisher=University of London}} located in the northern and central parts of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and the southern and central parts of {{ill|Manchuria|en|Manchuria|qid=Q81126|lt=Manchuria|1=Manchuria}}. At its peak of power, Goguryeo controlled most of the Korean peninsula, large parts of Manchuria and parts of eastern {{ill|Inner Mongolia|en|Inner Mongolia|qid=Q41079|lt=Inner Mongolia|1=Inner Mongolia}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Goguryeo/|title=Goguryeo|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|access-date=2019-04-14}} Along with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, Goguryeo was one of the {{ill|Three Kingdoms of Korea|en|Three Kingdoms of Korea|qid=Q165292|lt=Three Kingdoms of Korea|1=Three Kingdoms of Korea}}. In the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Goguryeo was often in {{ill|Military history of Goguryeo|en|Military history of Goguryeo|qid=Q17052201|lt=military conflict|1=Military history of Goguryeo}} with the Sui and Tang dynasties of China. Its relations with Baekje and Silla were complex and alternated between alliances and enmity. In the summer of 666, King [[:en:Yeon Gaesomun|Yeon Gaesomun]] died and Goguryeo was thrown into chaos and weakened by a succession struggle among his sons and younger brother. Tang {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|en|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|qid=Q9703|lt=Emperor Gaozong|1=Emperor Gaozong of Tang}} saw this as an opportunity and invaded, conquering the kingdom with the help of Silla by 668 AD. However, there was much resistance to Tang rule. Prince Go Yak-gwang (高若光), son of the last king of Goguryeo, {{ill|Bojang of Goguryeo|en|Bojang of Goguryeo|qid=Q498974|lt=Bojang|1=Bojang of Goguryeo}}, sent to Japan in 666 AD to seek military assistance from the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}. After the fall of the kingdom, he remained in exile in Japan. In 716 AD he was granted Koma County in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}, and settled in this location with 1799 Goguryeo refugees, mostly from {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|qid=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}}.{{cite news |url=http://newsletter.kf.or.kr/english/contents.asp?vol=110&lang=English&no=1369 |publisher=[[Korea Foundation]] |date=February 2009 |title=Finding Traces of Goguryeo Culture in Japan |last=Choi |first=Hyun-soo}} This territory included all of present-day Hidaka and {{ill|Tsurugashima, Saitama|en|Tsurugashima, Saitama|qid=Q869027|lt=Tsurugashima|1=Tsurugashima, Saitama}}, as well as parts of {{ill|Hannō|en|Hannō|qid=Q850472|lt=Hannō|1=Hannō}}, {{ill|Kawagoe, Saitama|en|Kawagoe, Saitama|qid=Q319740|lt=Kawagoe|1=Kawagoe, Saitama}}, {{ill|Iruma, Saitama|en|Iruma, Saitama|qid=Q696925|lt=Iruma|1=Iruma, Saitama}} and [[:en:Sayama, Saitama|Sayama]]. The area continued to be ruled by the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|qid=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan|1=Koma clan}} until the end of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. The shrine is believed to have been founded around this time.{{cite news |publisher=[[Yonhap]] |title=Japan's emperor to visit Koma shrine related to ancient Korean kingdom |date=September 19, 2017 |access-date=September 20, 2017 |url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20170919000599}} It received a stipend of three ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|qid=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Ranked]] as a prefectural shrine (県社). Following the {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|qid=Q503585|lt=annexation of Korea by Japan|1=Korea under Japanese rule}} in 1905, the Koma Shrine was used as a symbol by Japanese government officials, including [[:en:Governor-General of Korea|Governor-General of Korea]] {{ill|Jirō Minami|en|Jirō Minami|qid=Q712893|lt=Jirō Minami|1=Jirō Minami}}, for the assimilation and unification of the Korean and Japanese people.{{cite book |title=Spaces of Possibility: In, Between, and Beyond Korea and Japan |publisher=[[University of Washington Press]] |isbn=9780295998527 |year=2016 |editor-first=Clark W. |editor-last=Sorensen |editor-first2=Andrea Gevurtz |editor-last2=Arai}} In 1934 the Koma Jinja Hosan-kai was founded in order to preserve and restore the shrine, headed by then-{{ill|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q3315484|lt=Minister of Colonial Affairs|1=Ministry of Colonial Affairs (Japan)}} {{ill|Hideo Kodama|en|Hideo Kodama|qid=Q859979|lt=Hideo Kodama|1=Hideo Kodama}}. Koma Shrine maintained cultural relevance [[:en:Post-war Japan|after World War II]], and it was written about by prominent Japanese authors such as {{ill|Eiji Yoshikawa|en|Eiji Yoshikawa|qid=Q221957|lt=Eiji Yoshikawa|1=Eiji Yoshikawa}} and {{ill|Ango Sakaguchi|en|Ango Sakaguchi|qid=Q132111|lt=Ango Sakaguchi|1=Ango Sakaguchi}}. The shrine has more recently been fashioned as a symbol of Japanese-Korean friendship, and is endorsed by the {{ill|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|en|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)|qid=Q9405654|lt=South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs|1=Ministry of Foreign Affairs (South Korea)}}. ==Tourism== Approximately 300,000 tourists visit the shrine annually. The shrine is located about 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|qid=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Komagawa Station|en|Komagawa Station|qid=Q910599|lt=Komagawa Station|1=Komagawa Station}}. ==Religious function== The shrine deifies a Prince Go Yak'gwang (高若光) of the {{ill|Koma clan|en|Koma clan|qid=Q5361634|lt=Koma clan|1=Koma clan}} (later known as Genbu Jakkō (玄武若光)). Yakgwang reportedly introduced the cultivation of [[:en:Silkworms|Silkworms]] and [[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]], leading to his deification as Koma Myojin following his death. Its hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' are believed to be his descendants.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zkkjpWYS8icC&q=%22jakko%22+%22koma%22&pg=PA45 |page=45 |title=The Architecture of Tokyo: An Architectural History in 571 Individual Presentations |first=Hiroshi |last=Watanabe |isbn=9783930698936 |year=2001 |publisher=Edition Axel Menges}} The shrine is currently presided over by Koma Fumiyasu, whom according to the ''Koma-shi keizu'' [[:en:Genealogical|Genealogical]] scroll is a sixty-second generation direct descendant of Jakkō. ==Koma clan residence== The {{nihongo|Koma clan residence|高麗家住宅|Koma-ke jyutaku}} located adjacent to the shrine precincts was built in the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} era (1596-1615). It is a thatch roofed {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|lt=''irimoya''|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}-gabled building and was designated as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category-inline|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} * [https://komajinja.or.jp official home page] { {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}} \ {{Commons category|Koma-jinja (Hidaka)}} {{wikidata link|Q10855289|co|commons:Category:Koma-jinja (Hidaka)|en|Koma Shrine|fa|معبد کوما|fr|Sanctuaire Koma|ja|高麗神社|ko|고마 신사|zh|高麗神社}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hidaka, Saitama]] [[Category:Immigration to Japan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea relations]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Saitama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
